events.text: Fill the repository most of the way and check: the filesystem is getting tight.
events.prose: Fill the repository with data until the filesystem runs tight.

events.text: Grow it online. The encrypted container and the filesystem expand while the containers keep running — zero downtime, zero unmount.
events.prose: Expand the repository online: the LUKS container and filesystem grow with containers running.

events.text: Verify from inside: same volume, double the room.
events.prose: Check the filesystem from inside the repo — the new size is live.

events.text: Now the other direction. Delete the big file — but watch: the pool does not get the space back yet. Deleted blocks stay reserved inside the image.
events.afterText: Freed inside the repo, still owned by the image.
events.prose: Delete the data inside the repository — note the pool does not shrink yet.

events.text: Trim hands those blocks back to the datastore pool, online. This is the step most setups forget.
events.afterText: Reclaimed. The pool can breathe again.
events.prose: Run repo trim to return the freed blocks to the shared pool — online, zero downtime.

events.text: Now stop doing this by hand. The policy enables automatic growth up to a ceiling you choose, plus scheduled trims.
events.afterText: Policy stored. The machine takes over from here.
events.prose: Set a size policy: auto-grow with an explicit ceiling, plus scheduled trim.

events.text: Read it back: auto-grow on, ceiling at eight gigabytes, trim on a schedule.
events.prose: Read the effective policy back — growth and trim are now automatic.

events.text: And the view that ties it together: storage health shows every repo's usage, the pool, and how much copy-on-write sharing is saving you.
events.afterText: One view: usage, savings, and room to grow.
events.prose: Check machine storage health: per-repo usage, pool state, and CoW reflink savings.

narrations.text: Welcome to the final tutorial! Let's make sure you never run out of disk again. We'll grow a full repository online, hand deleted space back to the pool, and then teach the machine to do both automatically.

Here's the model. Every repository is a file in one shared datastore pool. Repositories can grow online while the containers keep running. But deleting data inside a repo frees nothing by itself. Trim is what hands the blocks back to the pool. And a policy automates both.
And that's a wrap on the series! Eighteen tutorials, from install to autopilot storage. Your servers are yours again. Thanks for watching!
